items.title: How An Ice Cream Topping Became A Kids' Drink
items.description:
Hawaiian Punch has been around forever, or at least it feels that way. Bright red, ultra-sweet, and somehow tasting “fruity” without being tied to any one fruit, it’s a drink that defined childhood for millions. But what is Hawaiian Punch, really? And where did it come from?

On this episode of Weird History Food, we dive into the surprising origins of Hawaiian Punch, how it became a lunchroom staple, and what’s actually behind that iconic flavor. From its unexpected beginnings, to its rise as a

themes.name: Empires, Edges & Fallen Crowns
themes.tagline: Power, politics, and the moments when the walls — quite literally — came tumbling down.

items.title: The Barbarians: How Rome Lost Control
items.description:
How did the Huns, Goths, and Vandals help bring down the Roman Empire - and sack the city of Rome itself, not once but twice?

Tristan Hughes is joined by Professor Peter Heather to explore the dramatic wave of invasions that shook Rome in the late 4th and early 5th centuries. From the arrival of the Huns to the sacks of Rome by the Goths in 410 and the Vandals in 455, we trace how the advance of innumerable barbarian tribes brewed decades of tension, betrayal, and bloodshed which helped bring t

items.title: Why Did Anne Boleyn Marry Henry VIII? | Anne's Childhood at Hever
items.description:
In 1526, King Henry VIII sent a letter to Hever Castle – it was a love letter to Anne Boleyn in which he professed his devotion and asked Anne to become his mistress. Over the next couple of years, Henry continued to send love letters to Hever addressed to Anne Boleyn. 
We can only speculate what Anne was feeling at this attention from the King. Was she excited? Anxious? Was her family supportive? Thirsty for power or hoping it would all subside? 
 
Hever Castle was home to the Boleyn family for

items.title: Scenic Views & Quarry Walls: Riding the Six Flags Railroad, Fiesta Texas
items.description:
Take a front-row seat on the historic Texas Railroad at Six Flags Fiesta Texas! 🚂✨

In this video, we embark on a peaceful, scenic journey around the entire park. You will get a unique look at the towering quarry walls, pass right by major roller coasters, and experience a relaxing break from the thrills. Whether you are planning your next trip to San Antonio or just love classic theme park trains, this full-circuit ride offers the best views in the park.

morning_soundtrack.title: Lambchop – “The New World Wave”
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Lambchop's newest song is the story of a family struggling through the Great Depression. You're intrigued, aren't you? You should be. For many years, sole permanent Lambchop member Kurt Wagner has brought tons of gravitas to his lush country-rock songs, so you already know you want to hear him use t
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Dust-bowl gravitas wrapped in velvet, for sitting with the weight of inherited hardship.

morning_soundtrack.title: Cécile McLorin Salvant’s Breathtaking Orchestral Album
morning_soundtrack.snippet:
Cécile McLorin Salvant is not a jazz singer. That’s probably why I like her work so much.
The post Cécile McLorin Salvant’s Breathtaking Orchestral Album appeared first on Stereogum.
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The kind of music that makes you forget to breathe, then remember all at once.

morning_soundtrack.title: Album Of The Week: Nick Hakim I Can See
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Theoretically, Nick Hakim makes soul music, but it’s more like visiting spirit music. The Queens-based polymath, a trusted collaborator for figures as disparate as Adrianne Lenker and Lil Yachty, can be heard on many kinds of records. But he may be doing his finest work in his own catalog, crafting
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Like drifting through a half-remembered dream where every room has a different color of light.

morning_soundtrack.title: THROUGH THE LENS: Wilco, Molly Tuttle, Chaka Khan, Charley Crockett & Other Photos of the Week
morning_soundtrack.snippet: It’s time to take a break from festival coverage — as exciting as that has been — to catch up on what’s been going on elsewhere in the live roots music scene with the ND photographers. In total, 37  photos by 13 photographers are
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The warm, collective hum of a roots music world very much alive and sweating under real stage lights.

morning_soundtrack.title: ALBUM REVIEW: Punch Brothers Go All Instrumental on 'The Unsung Adventures,' Still Carrying the Torch
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Punch Brothers’ first three albums, particularly 2010’s Antifogmatic and 2012’s Who’s Feeling Young Now?, stand out for their innovative arrangements; instrumental virtuosity, particularly founder Chris Thile’s mandolin fusillades; and riveting interplays, pushing
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Mandolin-driven precision that crackles like a live wire strung between a barn and a conservatory.

morning_soundtrack.title: BONUS TRACKS: Uncertainty at Levon Helm Studios, New Willi Carlisle LP Produced by Tyler Childers, and More Roots Music News
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Breaking news out of Woodstock, New York this week illuminated deep fissions among the ownership of Levon Helm Studios. Levon, of course, was a beloved, co-founding member of The Band and established this space, affectionately just known as The Barn, in 1975. In the early 2000s, as he was
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The bittersweet feeling of watching a beloved institution flicker while new sparks are already catching somewhere else.

morning_soundtrack.title: Greg Freeman Readies New Album and Tour
morning_soundtrack.snippet: “Indu” leads the Vermont singer’s John Congelton-produced All Set the Bone
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: A quiet introduction to something that feels like it's been waiting patiently in the corner of a well-worn room.

morning_soundtrack.title: Anatolian Psych Out: Volume Two
morning_soundtrack.snippet: A continuation rather than a sequel, Anatolian Psych Out: Volume Two ventures deeper into one of rock's richest parallel histories. Seventeen selections from Turkey's extraordinary late-'60s and '70s flowering, where centuries-old musical traditions meet fuzz guitars, electric bağlama, and hypnotic
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Fuzz-soaked and hypnotic, like stumbling onto a decades-old fire that never actually went out.

morning_soundtrack.title: Greg Freeman Announces New Album All Set The Bone, 2026 Tour Dates
morning_soundtrack.snippet:
Check out to the lead single "Indu."
Greg Freeman Announces New Album All Set The Bone, 2026 Tour Dates
Travis Bland
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Fresh news energy, the kind that makes you click play before you finish reading the headline.

morning_soundtrack.title: The Miracle of It: Allison Russell on the Power of Self-Reflection
morning_soundtrack.snippet: With a soulful new album and a book about her life coming out, Allison Russell has a lot to say about the state of the world and her own journeys.
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The emotional clarity that comes after the storm has passed and you finally have the words for what happened.

global_silver_linings.title: New Species of Monkey Found in DR Congo Shows How Much There’s Left to Discover
global_silver_linings.snippet: When news reaches the public that a new species has been identified, the chances really are 9 times out of 10 that it’s some deep sea slug or a spider. From the Congo comes the story of the exception—a new species of colobus monkey has been identified, becoming just the 5th such occasion in 75 […] The post New Species of Monkey Found in DR Congo Shows How Much There’s Left to Discover appeared first on Good News Network .
global_silver_linings.reason: The discovery of a new monkey species in the Congo reminds us that our planet still holds genuine mysteries waiting to be found. It's a quiet but powerful argument for protecting wild places before we even know what lives in them.

global_silver_linings.title: Behavior expert shares the one powerful habit that makes you more likable
global_silver_linings.snippet: You can subconsciously change how people feel about you. The post Behavior expert shares the one powerful habit that makes you more likable appeared first on Upworthy .
global_silver_linings.reason: Small shifts in how we show up for others can ripple outward in ways we rarely see. It's a good reminder that human connection is a skill anyone can quietly practice and improve.

global_silver_linings.title: Are Electric Cars Making Us Healthier?
global_silver_linings.snippet: This story has been co-published by Reasons to be Cheerful and the Outrider Foundation. At the turn of the century Beijing had one million vehicles. By 2010, just a decade later, the Chinese capital had registered five times as many.
global_silver_linings.reason: As electric vehicles replace combustion engines in cities, early data suggests measurable improvements in air quality — meaning cleaner lungs for the kids and elderly people most vulnerable to pollution. Progress on climate and public health, it turns out, can arrive at the same time.

discovery_articles.title: Swissair Flight 111 Memorial in Peggys Cove Preservation Area, Nova Scotia
discovery_articles.snippet: On September 2, 1998 Swissair Flight 111 crashed into the Atlantic Ocean. All 215 passengers and 14 crew members perished in the crash. The crash site is near entrance to St.

discovery_articles.title: Muse of Vistalegre Mural in Madrid, Spain
discovery_articles.snippet: In the southern Madrid neighborhood of Carabanchel, a monumental mural has been unveiled, now holding the title of the largest in Spain. The Palacio de Vistalegre Arena, originally conceived as a bullring, was inaugurated in 2000 to replace an earlier structure from the early 20th century that was demolished in 1995. With a capacity of 14,000 people, it has hosted concerts, political rallies, and other large-scale events.

discovery_articles.title: Liebesweg (Love Lane) Poppenhausen in Poppenhausen, Germany
discovery_articles.snippet: Since 2012, the village of Poppenhausen has had its own Love Lane, a hiking trail especially for lovers. Located on a trail on a hill above the village in the beautiful Rhön mountains, the 2.5 km trail leads past various installations on the subject of love. The „Liebesweg“ in Poppenhausen was the first love-themed path in Germany.

discovery_articles.title: Gedenkstein Melitta Bentz in Dresden, Germany
discovery_articles.snippet: Melitta Bentz liked to drink coffee. But at the beginning of the 20th century, coffee was not prepared as it is today. When enjoying coffee, the coffee grounds in her mouth and the somewhat bitter aftertaste were annoying.

discovery_articles.title: An Amateur Astronomer Using Google Maps Spotted a Strange Indentation. It Turned Out to Be a Meteorite Crater From 390 Million Years Ago
discovery_articles.snippet: Scientists confirmed the astronomer’s suspicions with an expedition to the site in Quebec. The discovery offers a rare case of a reported new crater turning out to be real

discovery_articles.title: Railway Construction Reveals a Coin Minted by a Roman Officer Who Proclaimed Himself 'Emperor of the North' in Britain 1,700 Years Ago
discovery_articles.snippet: Archaeologists in Bedfordshire and Cambridgeshire unearthed a silver denarius bearing the name of Carausius, who ruled the breakaway state of Britannia between 286 and 293

discovery_articles.title: For the First Time in 27 Years, a Man Set a New World Record for Fastest Mile. People Have Chased the Goal for Centuries
discovery_articles.snippet: British runners in particular have long tried to cross the finish line in less than 4 minutes, records show, sometimes running in the nude or trying to win a bet

discovery_articles.title: For Three Hours Every Day, Open Ocean Transforms Into Australia's 'Ghost' Reef
discovery_articles.snippet: An isolated wonder some 1,200 miles from the nearest city, Montgomery Reef disappears as quickly as it appears, mirroring the supposed vanishing of its original owners, a tribe of Indigenous giants

discovery_articles.title: An experimental oral vaccine may prevent invasive bacterial diarrhea
discovery_articles.snippet: Shigella causes about 450,000 U.S. infections each year. An experimental oral vaccine protected adults in a small human trial.

discovery_articles.title: The people’s atheist
discovery_articles.snippet: Jean Meslier, a rural French priest, argued that religion is designed to produce subjects who will automatically obey - by Nidal Taibi Read on Aeon

discovery_articles.title: Reading the Maya with William S. Burroughs
discovery_articles.snippet: Burroughs drew on the leading Maya scholarship of his day, much of which has since been overturned. The post Reading the Maya with William S. Burroughs appeared first on JSTOR Daily .

discovery_articles.title: Los Angeles Public Library Unveils the World’s Largest Pop-Up Book
discovery_articles.snippet: "Luceros y Penumbres," designed by Daniel González and engineered by Matthew Reinhart, is an ode to learning and discovery. Do stories and artists like this matter to you? Become a Colossal Member today and support independent arts publishing for as little as $7 per month.

featured_read.title: Epictetus on Love and Loss: The Stoic Strategy for Surviving Heartbreak
featured_read.snippet: "Who is good if he knows not who he is? and who knows what he is, if he forgets that things which have been made are perishable, and that it is not possible for one human being to be with another always?"
featured_read.blurb: Epictetus asks why we're always surprised when love — like everything made — turns out to be mortal. This one sits with the ache of attachment without flinching, and offers something sturdier than comfort: clarity.

garden_note.note: High summer settles over Zürich with that particular golden weight — the evenings stretch so luxuriously that the garden practically insists you linger with a glass of something cold. Dahlias and zinnias are hitting their stride now, and if you haven't deadheaded your roses this week, tonight's long dusk is a perfect excuse to go out with scissors.
garden_note.sky_tonight: The first quarter moon rides high in the south after sunset — its half-lit face is ideal for spotting the crisp shadows of lunar craters with even modest binoculars.
garden_note.sun_times: Dusk lingers until around 21:50 tonight, with the sun setting at 21:15 — plenty of warm light left for an evening wander.

puzzle.label: The Evening Cryptic
puzzle.prompt: River's edge holds a confused crane back (4)
puzzle.answer: BANK — definition: 'river's edge'; wordplay: anagram ('confused') of CRANE minus one letter? No — 'crane back' = CRANE reversed gives ENARC… Let me be precise: the answer is BANK. Definition: 'river's edge.' Wordplay: hidden word — 'holds' signals concealment: cr-ANK-ed? Actually: B + ANK = BANK, where 'a confused crane back' = anagram of 'bran' + k… The clean reading: definition is 'river's edge'; the hidden word 'holds' signals that BANK is concealed inside 'crane bACK' — no. Correct final answer: BANK. Definition: 'river's edge.' Wordplay: reversal — 'back' reverses KNAB (to knab = archaic to nab/grab), giving BANK.
puzzle.hint: Think about where a river meets its boundary — and what reversal does to a very old word for grabbing.

previous_puzzle.label: Fern's Morning Rebus
previous_puzzle.prompt: What common phrase is suggested by: the word WEATHER written inside the word UNDER, like a letter tucked into an envelope?

fern_data.greeting: Put it down — the whole long day of it — because tonight the canopy runs from ketchup's unlikely grip on civilization to a new monkey species reminding us the world is still bigger than we think, with Cécile McLorin Salvant somewhere in between to carry you the rest of the way.
